data.title: IJH: Core Mid-Cap Equities As A Foundation For A Diversified Portfolio Strategy
data.description:
The iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) is a passively managed, low-cost exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with broad exposure to US mid-cap equities. The strategy is widely adopted as a core equity strategy with over $100b in net assets and provides substantial trading depth for active portfolio rotation. IJH can be utilized by investors as the foundation for building a portfolio, actively rotating out of IJH into satellite positions as new investment opportunities arise.

About iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF

IJH was launched by iShares on May 22, 2000 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. IJH has a low expense ratio of 5bps and pays out a modest distribution rate of $0.83/share with a 30-day SEC yield of 1.21%. IJH is benchmarked to the S&P Mid-Cap 400 Index as provided by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

The Index largely covers US mid-cap equities with a market capitalization of $7.4-$20.5b at the time of inclusion. The Index weighs constituents based on market capitalization, meaning that larger mid-cap companies will have a bigger influence on the Index when compared to smaller companies. For example, the top 10 holdings account for 7.65% of the total portfolio weight whereas the bottom 10 holdings make up 0.43% of net assets. Though the top holdings make up a larger weight, the level of concentration risk is relatively minimal, particularly when compared to large-cap indices like the S&P 500 (SPX) where the top 10 constituents account for 39.51% of the portfolio weight. Given that the top holding in IJH, Comfort Systems USA (FIX), has a portfolio weight of roughly 1.07%, I do not believe any single holding will have substantial influence over the performance of the portfolio.

IJH is heavily weighted in industrials, making up 23.72% of the portfolio weight. Other large sector weightings include financials at 16.38%, information technology at 13.69%, and consumer discretionary at 11.65%. By comparison, the S&P 500 has a sector concentration of 35.29% in technology, 12.79% in financials, and 10.98% in communications. Given the sector weights, I believe IJH provides substantially more sector diversification while limiting exposure to the top constituents.

Looking at peer mid-cap strategies, investors may consider the State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 400 Mid Cap ETF (SPMD) or the Vanguard Mid Cap Index Fund ETF (VO) as alternatives to IJH, each offering a slightly lower expense ratio with a relatively similar strategy.

VO will be a more concentrated investment strategy with 293 individual holdings, far below IJH and SPMD. Given that we’re considering IJH as a core position, I believe diversification will be a critical factor for fund selection as equity or sector concentration can be added through additional investment strategies.

Investor Suitability

IJH was designed to be used as a core mid-cap strategy, meaning that IJH can be used as a foundational position in a portfolio with satellite positions added to adjust equity or sector concentration. Unlike the S&P 500, IJH will provide investors with a larger concentration in the industrials sector which may be appealing for investors seeking non-AI-related investments.

Though IJH pays out a quarterly distribution rate annualized at $0.83/share, I do not believe the 1.26% yield necessarily makes the portfolio strategy appealing from an income perspective.

I believe the best way to utilize IJH is to position one’s equity strategy around IJH. From a tactical perspective, investors may utilize IJH as a placeholder for unallocated funds in their equity portfolio, rotating funds from IJH to other ETFs or equities to gain exposure to other markets that may be underrepresented in IJH. Taking into consideration sector weightings, investors may consider adding energy or communications exposure by adding an ETF or equity investments.

IJH can also be used for macroeconomic traders that may utilize a more thematic approach to making investment decisions. For example, mid-cap equities may outperform large-caps during an interest rate reduction cycle as these companies, particularly those in the industrial sector, may be more sensitive to the cost of financing debt.

IJH can be used for both long-term and short-term investment horizons. IJH has roughly $100b in net assets with an average of $657mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors with substantial liquidity and trading depth for actively managing a position.

IJH is a passively managed strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the S&P Mid-Cap 400 Index. Given that this is a passively managed strategy, investors will be responsible for tactical positioning as the fund will not adjust the strategy based on market conditions. Sector concentration may limit growth potential for IJH given the smaller allocation to the technology sector.

Final Thoughts

IJH can be an appealing core mid-cap equity holding for investors seeking to actively manage a portfolio strategy. IJH can best be utilized as a foundation or a placeholder for capital as an investor builds a portfolio of satellite positions around the core equity holding.

This article answers these three main questions about IJH:

  • Does IJH offer diversification away from large-cap tech names that dominate the broader indices?
  • Is IJH actively managed or passively managed?
  • What type of investor is IJH most suitable for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: TIC Solutions: Upside Is Visible, But The Company Needs To Prove Itself
data.description:
TIC Solutions (TIC) is a service company dedicated to testing, inspection and certification services and engineering. In its current form, TIC is a combination of two companies, Acuren and NV5 Global, and their integration has just begun. TIC Solutions is focused on energy, infrastructure and industrial end markets.

TIC Solutions reminds me of an investment case on Atlas Technical Consultants, which I published in 2022. Atlas Technical Consultants was taken public as a SPAC and it had a little messy financials, high debt and a series of acquisitions behind it. Like TIC Solutions, Atlas was engaged in testing, inspection and certification and technical design. In 2023, the company was acquired by a private equity firm which paid 124% premium and EV/EBITDA multiple of 11.5x.

Also Acuren was listed through a SPAC in 2025. Similarly to Atlas, many of its services are mandatory to its customers due to regulation and compliance, therefore a large part of its revenue is sticky and recurring. As a service provider, its business does not tie up capital and delivers strong cash flow. Both companies have a strong track record of acquisitive growth and resilience over cycles. The early results of the integration of the two companies are promising and the shares have upside if the company can meet the higher end of its guidance.

More diversified combination of two equals

TIC Solutions has three main segments, Inspection & Mitigation, Consulting Engineering and Geospatial. Most of its $2 billion revenue comes from the U.S., primarily from customers in infrastructure, energy and industrial sectors. One fifth of the business originates from the public-sector. TIC Solutions has 11 000 employees and over 250 locations. EY Parthenon estimates the global testing, inspection and certification market to grow 4-5% annually.

In 2024, NV5 posted a revenue of approximately $1 billion, so the merger is a combination of almost equal sized companies. Acuren’s main service lines included non-destructive testing, rope access and engineering and laboratory testing, whereas NV5’s focus was on consulting engineering and geospatial services. Now, the new entity is more diversified in terms of end markets and services although NV5’s margins were lower than Acuren’s.

In the first nine months, TIC Solutions grew revenue by 5%. The third quarter came in slower, proforma revenue growth of 2.4%. In Q3, adjusted EBITDA margin declined by 0.6 %-points to 16.3% but was higher than YTD margin of 15.4%. On top of the merger, TIC Solutions has made four bolt-on acquisitions this year, which support future growth.

The company is in a show-me situation

In the merger the owners of NV5 received cash and shares worth $23 per share, a 32% premium. Including the debt, the deal resulted in an enterprise value of $1.7 billion. The price paid equaled EV/EBITDA-multiple of approximately 12x. In 2024, NV5 grew revenue by 10% mainly thanks to acquisitions and adjusted EBITDA grew by 7%. It had a modest leverage ratio of 1.4x.

NV5 brought in Consulting Engineering and Geospatial revenue. However before the deal it pursued to highlight its testing, inspection and certification offering. Although NV5 did not disclose its revenue split by activity, this is most likely one of the main motivations for the merger. Now, NV5 revenue is grouped into two slices, Consulting Engineering and Geospatial, of the pie chart above describing the revenue split between the segments.

NV5’s businesses take the combined company closer to a couple of growth opportunities. Naturally, datacenters are one of them. Its sales to the datacenter sector “more than doubled” over the last 12 months. The sales to datacenters is only 3% of total revenue, but so far it is delivering only a limited amount of services to the sector, according to the Q3 earnings call. Expanding the offering should provide a long growth runway in the vertical.

TIC has highlighted the opportunities for cross-selling as a justification for the merger as TIC pursues to sell more services to the clients of each company. It has presented compelling case stories, however investors should remain cautious. Customers could already have their trusted partners in both lines of businesses and changing the service provider might be more difficult and take longer than initially expected.

TIC Solutions has launched some new services created by a combination of assets and expertise from the two merged companies. One such opportunity is expanding NV5’s services to Canada, where Acuren generated 43% of its revenue. Surprisingly fast TIC has come up and implemented entirely new offerings that combine NV5’s capabilities and Acuren’s field force.

Integration of Acuren and NV5 has just begun. Apparently, the early results are promising. TIC raised its near-term cost synergy target from $20 million to $25 million. Also, the company expects to achieve synergies faster than originally communicated.

Traditionally most of the testing, inspection and certification firms, such as Eurofins, Intertek, UL Solution and Bureau Veritas, are pure plays. Now, many investors might be confused by the Consulting Engineering segment of TIC. The management needs to do better work communicating what it includes as benchmarking and peer valuation could appear more problematic. It is good to remember that the above mentioned peers have greater focus on products, especially going into the consumer market. TIC Solutions is more focused on more industrial customers and utilities, which require field work for instance.

High debt and negative earnings might repel some investors

The merger and high debt led TIC Solutions to issue new shares through a private placement to an existing shareholder. In October, it issued 20.8 million shares worth $250 million at a price of $12 per share. In other words, the dilution was approximately 10%. On the other hand, share issuance helped the company with its debt position. In the future, TIC should be able to de-lever thanks to its good cash flow generation.

The acquisition of NV5 increased TIC’s leverage ratio to 4.4x, while the target ratio is 3x. It has long-term debt of $1.6 billion without maturities in the near future. Due to significant interest expense depreciation and amortization and transaction costs, TIC Solutions reported negative earnings for the first nine months. As long as TIC reports negative earnings the stock remains in the shadows to many investors.

Fairly valued but upside is found in an optimistic scenario

The management has given certain parameters for 2026. They expect revenue growth of 3% to 5% and adjusted EBITDA-margin of 15.5% to 16.5%. It would mean revenue of approximately $2.17 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $330 million, calculated by lower range figures. This would mean EV/EBITDA multiple of 11x. These figures are for the combined entity as 2026 will be a year with full NV5 impact. For 2026, analysts are expecting revenue of $2.21 billion so in order to meet the estimates TIC needs to deliver figures closer to the upper end of its guidance.

If we play with the most optimistic figures of management’s guidance, significant upside emerges. If TIC Solutions meets the upper end of its revenue ($2.15 billion) guidance this year, delivers 5% revenue growth in 2026 and reaches 16.5% EBITDA-margin, the share price would reach $11.7, assuming $1.5 billion net debt, no change in the multiple or the number of shares outstanding. Assuming EV/EBITDA-multiple would increase to 12x, share price would be $13.3.

On a forward EV/EBITDA basis TIC Solutions trades in line with the other testing, inspection and certification peers according to Koyfin. The group’s average EV/EBITDA multiple stands exactly at 10.3x according to Koyfin. The French Eurofins has the lowest ratio of 8.3x and UL Solutions the highest, 20.3x. Is TIC Solutions as good business as the average? Based on EBITDA-margin it is not. TIC Solutions should improve its margin by 4-5 %-points to meet the level of its peers. In addition, when looking at the acquisition multiples of NV5, previously mentioned Atlas Technical Consultants or Spanish Applus, it is difficult to see large potential for multiple expansion.

Lately, analyst following has increased from two to four. The target prices range from $11.5 to $17 averaging a little below $14.

Conclusion

TIC Solutions operates in attractive and sticky business lines. For many investors the investment case could be in a wait-and-see mode as the merger proceeds. At the moment, TIC Solutions appears neutrally valued. The potential upside relies on better than expected sales and cost synergies. A rather unique combination needs to prove its value. In a scenario where the company delivers market growth, improves slightly its profitability and perhaps pays down debt, investors can achieve a significant price appreciation.

data.title: Datadog: Multiproduct Sales And AI Deals Underpin Strong Growth (Upgrade)
data.description:
As we approach the end of the year, we look back at a very volatile 2025 that has seen a lot of upheaval in the stock markets, especially as the growth stock category experienced a change of leadership. In particular, many of the enterprise software stocks that led COVID-era gains have pulled back and underperformed the S&P 500, despite having AI tailwinds of their own.

Datadog (DDOG), the infrastructure monitoring software stock, is up ~10% this year, underperforming the S&P 500 but well ahead of many battered enterprise software peers like HubSpot (HUBS) and Atlassian (TEAM), where AI is considered to be a bigger threat. Amid strong recent results for Datadog, however, we think the company is on the cusp of regaining its momentum.

I last wrote an article upgrading Datadog to a "Neutral" rating in September, when the stock was trading around $140. Recently, on Nov. 6, Datadog reported a strong Q3 beat-and-raise, which immediately prompted a 20%+ rally to ~$200: gains that were quickly reversed, even though the clear demand tailwinds that Datadog is showcasing appear to be well sustained. I view the volatile pullback as a great opportunity to buy Datadog as it looks to push higher, and I'm upgrading the stock to a "Buy" rating.

For investors who are newer to Datadog, here is what I consider to be the long-term bull case for this stock:

  • Clear land and expand momentum. Unlike subscription-based software companies, Datadog's revenue ultimately depends on customers' consumption of its products. Despite clear IT budget moderation this year, Datadog has boosted its net revenue retention rates to ~120%, reflecting an average customer who boosts its spending by ~20%.
  • Multiproduct sales and tailwinds from AI and non-AI customers. Deal momentum from AI companies remains a core reason that Datadog can sustain near-30% revenue growth despite its >$3 billion annualized revenue scale, but the company notes that standard non-AI customers are also upgrading their packages and purchasing multiple products on the Datadog platform.
  • Rule of 50. Forget the "Rule of 40" - Datadog combines a mid-20s operating margin with a high-20s revenue growth rate, putting Datadog's Rule of 40 score above 50.
  • Growing cloud security opportunity. More and more, customers are turning to Datadog's monitoring capabilities as a security solution. The company believes the TAM for cloud security is growing at a 17% CAGR, which is double the 9% expected CAGR in observability, while Datadog's near-30% revenue growth (eclipsing growth in both markets) suggests that Datadog is taking share.

Meanwhile, there are two main risks to Datadog that I'm tracking closely. The first is that Datadog is investing heavily into sales and marketing to capture as much business as it can in the AI age, which is reflected in a slight y/y decay in operating margins (which I think is more than justified, as Datadog has managed to maintain healthy growth rates and its Rule of 50 positioning).

The second is a premium valuation. At current share prices near $157, Datadog trades at a market cap of $53.96 billion. Netting off the $4.14 billion of cash and $982.4 million of convertible debt on Datadog's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $50.80 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, Datadog has substantially boosted its full-year revenue outlook to $3.385-$3.39 billion, or 26% y/y growth, versus 24% y/y growth expected previously. Its operating margin guidance, meanwhile, now reflects a 22% full-year margin.

For FY26, Street estimates point to $4.10 billion in revenue for Datadog, reflecting 21% y/y growth. This positions Datadog's valuation multiples at:

  • 15.1x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 12.4x EV/FY26 revenue

I now consider Datadog to be in the "middle of the pack" when it comes to mid-20s growth software companies. There are penalty-box stocks like Atlassian (TEAM) and GitLab (GTLB) that are growing at a similar pace and yet trade at much cheaper multiples (due to the prevalent market narrative that vibe coding will take away software developer jobs that are the main paid seats on these platforms). That said, there are also still stocks like ServiceNow (NOW) and JFrog (FROG) that trade in line with Datadog:

Considering Datadog's ability to showcase strong high-20s growth without any deceleration in a tough budget environment, plus deliver improvements in net retention rate, I think Datadog still leaves room for further upside. Stay long here.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Datadog's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Datadog's revenue grew 28% y/y to $885.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $851.3 million (+23% y/y) by a huge five-point margin. We note as well that Datadog's revenue growth didn't decelerate at all versus 23% y/y growth in Q2. The company has demonstrated a very conservative slant in its guidance: its latest outlook calls for growth to fall to ~24% in Q4 as well, but given Datadog's own history, we expect the company to outperform.

Catalyzing strong growth for the company is the growing trend for customers to adopt multiple products on the Datadog platform, especially its cloud security solutions. The share of customers that are using more than 8 products at Datadog has grown to 16% of its total customer base, 4 points higher y/y. Meanwhile, customers using 6+ products has grown to 31% of the customer base, up 7 points from 24% of customers last year.

Perhaps most meaningful is the fact that after Datadog saw net retention rates slide to the ~mid 110s last year, the company has clocked in at a ~120% net retention rate over the past two quarters. We note that many enterprise software companies have noted difficulties with net retention this year amid budget caution. HubSpot (HUBS), for example, is barely showcasing any net expansion. Datadog's improvement in net retention despite a tough macro climate showcases that the company's investments into sales are paying off.

Per CEO Olivier Pomel's remarks on the Q3 earnings call regarding go-to-market momentum, which was spread across both AI companies and non-AI business:

We have seen broad-based positive trends in the demand environment with an ongoing strength of cloud migration and digital transformation. Against this backdrop, we executed a very strong Q3 both in new logo bookings and usage growth of existing customers.

As a notable inflection, we saw acceleration of year-over-year revenue growth across our non-AI customers. And the sequential usage growth for non-AI existing customers was the highest we have seen going back 12 quarters. This growth was broad-based as our customers are adopting more products and getting more value from the Datadog platform.

We also experienced strong revenue growth for our AI native customers and a broadening contribution to growth among those customers. There, too, we saw an acceleration of growth in our AI cohort in Q3 when excluding our largest customer.

Looking at new business. Contribution from new customers increased in Q3 in both the amount of new customer bookings as well as the revenue contribution from new customers. And as usual, churn has remained low with gross revenue retention stable in the mid- to high 90s, highlighting the mission-critical nature of our platform for our customers.

That said, we are mindful of the fact that the company has stepped up its sales spending, with sales and marketing costs growing 25% y/y to $199.5 million, excluding stock comp. R&D spending is also jumped 38% y/y to $269.5 million.

As a result, Datadog's pro forma operating margins pulled back 2 points y/y to 23%, which still puts Datadog firmly in the "Rule of 50" camp. We note that the company's long-term operating model calls for pro forma operating margins to stabilize above 25%, though we note that the company's current levels of cash flow and profitability aren't yet enough to justify the stock's valuation and move it off a revenue multiples-based assessment.

Key takeaways

With investors chasing the hottest AI stocks in the 2025 stock market, many buying opportunities have actually opened up in former Wall Street darlings that were too expensive to buy during the COVID era. Datadog has underperformed the S&P 500 this year, and is still below lows of ~$200 briefly notched in November (and earlier, in 2021). With healthy net retention rates, excellent growth/profitability balance as demonstrated by its Rule of 50 positioning, and strong deal momentum, I'm willing to look past Datadog's slightly rich valuation multiples and invest for the long haul.

data.title: C.H. Robinson Worldwide: Overlooked AI Beneficiary
data.description:
The market has been hyperventilating on AI for a while now (emphasis on while), and in my opinion, this is the most unloved and/or widely publicized bubble in recent memory.

I think both unloved and publicized attributes of this bubble can be traced to the fact that there are only a handful of beneficiaries of this bubble, and the majority of the market has shown little to no gains, even while attributing AI to be a total game-changer for our lives.

For my readers, I want to say this. Ignore the noise and be aware of the fact that there is a massive difference between building AI (which is capital-intensive and gets caught up in the bubble argument) and using AI (which is purported to increase productivity, be a net gain for the company's bottom line and can print cash)

Looking through that lens, a company that popped up on my radar would be C.H. Robinson Worldwide (CHRW).

But here is the kicker: unlike the prevailing promise in the market that companies will benefit from using AI and that the future looks bright, C.H. Robinson is using and benefiting from AI now.

C.H. Robinson Worldwide's Business Model

First, an introduction to the company and its operations.

C.H. Robinson is one of the world’s largest third-party logistics (3PL) providers. While logistics may conjure up images of trucks, ships or planes, the company's business model involves none of that (at least not directly), and they operate an asset-light model.

The company operates the network as a massive information exchange where they connect customers (businesses that need to move goods) with contract carriers (truckers, airlines, etc.). Charge the shipper a fee to move the goods, and pay the carrier a slightly lower fee to move the goods and pocket the spread.

Ultimately, the takeaway here is that customers outsource the headache of logistics to CHRW’s tech-powered network, while CHRW earns a fee on every shipment.

The proof is in the pudding

Part 1: Thriving where the industry is struggling

This is a new C.H. Robinson, and we don't use the macro environment as an excuse. We are a fundamentally different company than we were 2 years ago, illustrated by the company's consistent outperformance versus the market. But make no mistake, this consistent outperformance does not just happen, and it's not easy for others to replicate.

- David Bozeman, President, CEO and Director

It is well established by now that the goods transportation industry is in a recession and has been for some time. Many companies operating in the space are not only struggling, but some have gone bankrupt.

I pulled together a list of companies in the Logistics industry, and the results are unsurprising. Even our subject, C.H.Robinson has not fared well when you look at its revenues.

The list shows that most companies are showing flattish to negative growth, and only a few stocks/companies are showing a positive return. Strangely, among the stock return winners, C.H. Robinson showed a massive positive return while showing poor growth in its top-line. What gives?

Part 2: Improving bottom line, still rewarding shareholders

1. Despite a freight recession where revenues were down due to lower shipping rates, their earnings surged, beating analyst expectations.

2. TTM, they reported $16.5B in revenues, $1.4B in Gross profits and $0.8B in operating profits with a net income of $0.6B. Same time, 2 years ago, the numbers are $18.5B, $1.2B and $0.6B respectively, with a net income of $0.4B. This shows that margins have improved significantly.

3. They have used their robust cash flows to pay down their debt and deleverage their balance sheet. In my opinion, this is the most impressive aspect of the company. At a time when debt held by the companies in this space is spiralling and eventually ending in doom, C.H.Robinson is going in the opposite direction.

Our net debt-to-EBITDA leverage at the end of Q3 was 1.17x, down from 1.40x at the end of Q2. This financial strength is a key differentiator in our industry, giving us the ability to continue investing through the bottom of the freight cycle and further enhancing our capabilities.

- Damon Lee, CFO

4. The company has continued to engage in actions that are quite friendly to shareholders. For the latest quarter alone, they have returned $190M of cash through $115M of share repurchases and $75M of dividends. In fact, the company is an overlooked dividend champion that has consistently raised dividends over the last two decades, and they have committed to buybacks of $2B over the next three years (This buyback is on top of an existing authorization that still has 4.5M shares remaining)

Part 3: AI is the answer

A quote for a shipment from China to Charlotte, N.C., could take a worker several days because of the complexity of figuring out routes, carriers and customs paperwork. Agentic AI can compile the same quote in about an hour.

- CHRW CEO Dave Bozeman

Now comes the question that we will have to revisit. How is the company able to do this?

1. The company says it is using machine learning and generative artificial intelligence to automate tasks, with the result being higher and faster response times.

2. They have been able to automate tens of thousands of rate quotes/day and have generated about half of the company’s carrier bookings and appointments using AI.

Before AI: Able to respond to about 60% of quote requests in a day.

After AI: Ability to respond to every request

3. They have credited the technology for their drastic reduction in overall headcount numbers, while growing the customer-facing roles (the company ended June with 12.8K workers, down approximately 20% from two years earlier).

4. Since the end of 2022, C.H. Robinson has reported a net increase in productivity of over 40%.

Wrapping up, I want to leave readers with this thought. If the company is able to achieve this performance while facing a recession in its industry, how will things look when the freight market turns around?

Risks: What Could Go Wrong?

The Freight Cycle

The industry is still plagued by a recession, and the company has done a great job of navigating this through efficiency gains from Artificial Intelligence. If the global economy slows down further and ultimately results in a hard landing, shipping volumes could dry up further, and at the end of the day no amount of AI can fix a lack of customers.

Tech Disruption

While some of the legacy competitors could just be waking up to the use of AI for lean and efficient operations, the same thing may not be said of tech-first entrants such as Uber. If they build a better mousetrap, CHRW loses market share.

Currently, some of CHRW's competitors have been rated higher (in surveys conducted by Gartner) when it comes to service, support, ease of integration and deployment with better evaluation and contracting. I am not too worried yet, as it's still a minuscule sample compared to the size of their market, but if this starts to become representative of the overall market, it could become a real risk.

Valuation Compression

Out of all the risks, this is probably the one that is most obvious. By most measures, the stock appears to be overvalued, and the market continues to bid up the stock.

C.W. Robinson currently trades at a P/E of 32x, quite above the Industrials sector median, and if the market decides to treat CHRW as a boring utility rather than a tech-enabled earnings workhorse, the P/E multiple could contract much closer or even below the sector median (a 30-50% downside in that case cannot be ruled out).

In my opinion, for the stock to trade at this level and go up from here, the market is looking at one or several of the factors below, and every one of these factors could stay on for years to come.

1. Its earnings growth means its PEG ratio stands at 0.46, a highly attractive valuation metric for a company.

2. The AI-centric growth means it may not only squeeze more margins from its business but also at a prime position to capitalize when the top-line also turns upward. In this situation, the market wants to be in the driver's seat when this happens, buying into future growth.

3. The stock buybacks put a continuous bid on the stock, and in the end, it becomes a simple law of supply and demand.

The Past and the Future

I've seen gluts not followed by shortages, but I've never seen a shortage not followed by a glut.

- Nassim Nicholas Taleb

As I wrap up my analysis, I want to leave the readers with an analogy that has been in my mind for a while. Whether it plays out or not like the last time is up to anyone's guess, but I can't help shaking a very possible and potential scenario.

Rewinding to the dot-com mania, telecommunications firms became inebriated by the prospect of infinite internet growth. Multiple firms outcompeted each other to lay out fibre-optic cables across continents and under oceans. If we are looking at unlimited growth, any amount of supply is scarce. The formula was simple:

1. Build more capacity than anyone else

2. Scale faster than rivals

3. Dominate the future.

There is an estimate that up to $500B, financed mostly by debt, was invested in the infrastructure.

Of course, how many dared to ask the question whether the companies were building more capacity than the market actually needed?

The eventual bust of 2000-2002 meant the carnage was swift. Overleveraged companies went into bankruptcy, but you can probably guess where this is going next.

The fibre optics cable laid by these companies became the backbone of the internet for years to come, and the biggest beneficiaries were the users of this infrastructure, which was available at a fraction of the cost.

Fast forward to now, let's see what's happening in the market.

Trillion and billion-dollar companies are spending gobs of money, building AI models, training data centers, and acquiring GPUs with the reasoning that the future belongs to whoever accumulates the most compute. The competition, hype and overspend could be reminiscent of the dot-com era.

CEOs after CEOs, executives after executives, all have only one thing to complain about - that there is simply not enough AI infrastructure to go around.

Whether the capacity is excessive or not is only realized in hindsight, but hard not to see the parallels. The true winners may not be the ones in the infrastructure game, but it could be the companies with the massive, proprietary datasets that can plug cheap AI into their operations to unlock explosive efficiency.

As the technology becomes cheaper and gets commoditized, C.H. Robinson is already ahead of the curve in embedding them into existing logistics operations, and the improvement in models and cost means the company's moat only gets deeper and wider.

I believe C.H. Robinson is a logistics giant that is already a proven winner whose best years are ahead of it.

data.title: Overbought In A Tight Market: Seadrill's Valuation Overshadows Favorable Dynamics
data.description:
Since emerging from Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2022, Seadrill Limited’s (SDRL) efforts at capitalizing on the ongoing upcycle in offshore drilling have resulted in growing revenues, although profitability has still dragged. Current conditions, namely, the extensive contract backlog, rising average dayrates, and supply-side conditions, suggest that profitability will see a lift in future quarters. However, the market seems to have overbid the price, making this a Hold.

A Drillship Contractor

Seadrill owns and operates drillships and semi-submersible rigs, deploying them to provide high-specification drilling units and services to supermajors, state-owned national oil companies, and independent oil and gas companies around the world, who pay for services on a dayrate basis. Clients are signed to contracts which go on for months or even years, providing revenue visibility. In the first nine months of the year ended September 30, 2025, contract revenues accounted for 75.91% of total revenues. As of September 30, 2025, Seadrill had $2.5 billion in contract backlog, showing the strength of revenue growth that investors can expect.

The firm’s fleet of drillships and semi-submersible rigs operates in the deepwater and harsh environment market, with significant presence in the Gulf of Mexico, Brazil, West Africa, and the North Sea. The company also provides management services to some of its affiliated entities, which, in the nine months of the year ended September 30, 2025, accounted for 17.58% of total revenues. The company also earns revenue from procuring supplies, equipment, personnel, and other services on behalf of their clients; and from leasing out its drillships.

An Oligopoly

The offshore drilling industry is an oligopoly with high barriers to entry, primarily capital costs and technical expertise. Seadrill competes within a tiered market where fleet quality, operational performance, and financial stability are key differentiators. Its direct high-specification competitors are Valaris Limited (VAL), Noble Corporation plc (NE), and Transocean Ltd. (RIG), who all offer ultra-deepwater (UDW) and harsh-environment contracts. Diamond Offshore Drilling, Inc., another competitor, was acquired in September 2024 by Noble. Transocean is burdened by a debt/equity ratio of 0.77 compared to Seadrill’s 0.22, Valaris’ 0.48 and Noble’s 0.44. Seadrill’s financial strength is important in bidding for long-term contracts requiring financial surety. The company also competes with Helmerich & Payne, Inc. (HP) and Nabors Industries Ltd. (NBR) in the jackup and shallow water market. Finally, Seadrill competes with Expro Group Holdings N.V. (XPRO), Helix Energy Solutions Group, Inc. (HLX), Oceaneering International, Inc. (OII), Oil States International, Inc. (OIS), and RPC, Inc. (RES), who, while not being drilling contractors, provide essential services such as well intervention, subsea, and equipment.

Seadrill has admirable UDW assets, thanks to its modern fleet. As of November 5, 2025, Seadrill has a total fleet of 17 drilling units, with 13 units under active contracts, and four stacked. Of the total fleet of 17 drilling units, 16 are floaters -10 drillships and 6 semi-submersibles-. Within the operating units, three, West Gemini, Sonangol Libongos, and Sonangol Quenguela, are managed on behalf of the Angolan exploration company Sonangol E.P., for which Seadrill is paid through a daily management fee. The average age is significantly below industry leaders like Transocean, while its strong balance sheet is an important differentiator, allowing investment in upgrades without financial distress. However, Seadrill’s primary disadvantage is scale: compared to Seadrill’s 14 marketed rigs, Valaris has 60, while Noble has 309.

A Capital Intense Business

In the trailing twelve months (TTM), the firm’s drilling units made up 73.67% of total assets, and 229.23% of revenue, demonstrating the capital intensity of the business model. Capital intensive industries are particularly prone to boom-and-bust cycles, or what economists call the “cobweb model”: it takes a long time between ordering new drilling units and observing the prices at which they arrive on the market. So, in boom times, it is easy for managers to overestimate future prices, order more capital expenditure than is needed, leading to excess supply, which, in turn, drives prices and returns down until the clearing price is reached. The best time to invest is not when capacity is rising, but when capacity is being destroyed. It is then that future prices and returns are at their highest.

Inelastic Supply

The global drillship fleet is fixed in the short-to-medium term. S&P Global’s Petrodata Weekly Rig Count, which includes all jackups, semi-submersibles, and drillships, shows that global market utilization is at 88.9%, and that marketed supply has declined from 615 last year to 592 last week, while total supply has declined from 696 last year to 671 last week. Seadrill has even more granular data, which shows that of the world’s 85 drillship units, the four largest UDW drillships are responsible for 73% of the world’s competitive drillship supply. In the calendar year 2025, just 9 rigs were added to the global drillship supply.

Seadrill believes that there is just one stranded asset undelivered, with “high capital costs, limited yard availability and long lead times reducing the likelihood of newbuilds.” The capital cost of a newbuild UDW drillship is in excess of $600 million, with a 2 to 3 year construction lead time. With uncertainty around oil prices and the speed of the energy transition, speculative newbuilding is unlikely. Competition is unlikely to be affected by market entrants, and existing capacity will remain fairly stable.

Inelastic supply means that if dayrates rise, supply cannot respond fast enough to affect the current supply dynamics, which are baked in for the short-to-medium term. Such conditions are favourable for price increases, assuming that demand rises faster than supply. In Seadrill’s favour is that, post-restructuring, it has maintained a young, technically advanced fleet, that allows it to command premium dayrates for complex, deepwater, and harsh-environment work.

Sharply Declining Financial Performance

Since 2022, Seadrill’s revenue has grown from $936 million to $1.3 billion in the TTM. Revenue, which is driven by average dayrates and fleet utilization, will continue to grow. With the global stacked fleet absorbed, dayrates have begun a sharp ascent. Seadrill’s recent contract awards have been at rising average drillship dayrates that, in 2025, will be higher than in 2024.

Seadrill’s GAAP net income has declined from $3.9 billion to $34 million. This is, however, distorted by the $3.58 billion gain on settlement of liabilities that the company earned in 2022. In that time, operating income has declined from $72 million to $$34 million as a result of rising vessel and rig operating expenses, which have gone up in response to inflationary pressures. Those inflationary pressures remain.

Valuation Leaves Room For Downside

At the current price, Seadrill has an EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.69 compared to a sector median of 7.22. The previously mentioned peers have an average EV/EBITDA of 5.79. Assuming that EBITDA per share remains at $3.98, then, if the company’s EV/EBITDA multiple converges on that of the sector and peers, there is a downside of 16.35%, for a target price of $25.86.

EV/EBITDA Multiple Source

Target Price = EBITDA per Share Estimate × EV/EBITDA

Upside/(Downside) from $30.92

Sector Median

$28.71

-7.15%

Peer Group Average

$23.02

-25.55%

Equally Weighted Target

$25.86

-16.35%

Source: Seeking Alpha and author calculations.

The most obvious catalyst for convergence is from disappointing earnings reports, such as it has had. Macroeconomic conditions could worsen, leading to a broad sector de-rating, which would particularly affect Seadrill.

Conclusion

The supply-side conditions for elevated earnings are good. The industry structure is favorable, newbuilds are unlikely, and average dayrates and Seadrill’s backlog suggest further growth and improved earnings are likely. However, the market seems to have overbid the price and now, there are meaningful downsides in owning the stock. Overall conditions make this a Hold rather than a Sell.

data.title: Silver And Gold: Will Miner ETFs Shine Beyond The Holidays?
data.description:
Silver and Gold Shining Brighter Than Ever

Momentum in precious metals has accelerated in 2025, with silver recently breaking through the psychological $50 per ounce level to a new all-time high and more than doubling in value this year. Gold has surged alongside it, pacing its best annual performance since 1979, and analysts are projecting 20% to 25% more implied upside, based on price targets of $4,900 to $5,000 for 2026.

Fed Funds Futures now assign nearly 90% probability of a rate cut on Dec. 10, a shift that historically benefits metals. Riding this momentum, silver and gold miner ETFs rank among 2025’s strongest performers with gains from 140% to 170%. The historical performance of my five silver and gold ETFs is impressive, with an average year-to-date price performance of 153%.

While this momentum may continue in 2026, investors should remember that miners behave like leveraged exposure, amplifying gains of the underlying metal but also magnifying downside risk.

How I Chose the Best Silver and Gold ETFs

To select the best silver and gold ETFs, I started my search using the Seeking Alpha ETF Screener and then began to use a preselected screen, the Top Sector Equity ETFs. This identified the highest Quant-rated exchange-traded funds. I then reviewed the 10 highest-rated precious metals ETFs and narrowed my list to five, specifically focusing on those with emphasis on high grades in momentum, expenses, and liquidity factors.

1. iShares MSCI Global Silver and Metals Miners ETF (SLVP)

  • AUM: $675M
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Ranked in Asset Class (as of 12/3/2025): 1 out of 445
  • Ranked in Sub Class (as of 12/3/2025): 1 out of 123
  • Expense Ratio: 0.39%

SLVP provides targeted exposure to 30 global silver-mining companies by tracking the MSCI ACWI Select Silver Miners Investable Index, making it one of the purest equity plays on silver prices. Its appeal lies in its broad international diversification, including allocations to Canada, Mexico and select emerging markets where production growth is accelerating. As for key factors, SLVP’s high AUM and low fees vs. peers contribute to its high scores in liquidity and expenses, respectively. SLVP also leads its peer group for performance, contributing to its A+ momentum score.

SLVP vs. Silver Miner Peers: YTD Performance

Compared with larger, more concentrated silver miner ETFs, SLVP has fewer double-digit allocations to its top 10 holdings. This offers a more balanced factor profile, which can help reduce volatility while still capturing silver’s powerful upside. These traits set the stage for my next silver miner fund, which is more concentrated.

2. Global X Silver Miners ETF (SIL)

  • AUM: $4.20B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Ranked in Asset Class (as of 12/3/2025): 3 out of 445
  • Ranked in Sub Class (as of 12/3/2025): 3 out of 12
  • Expense Ratio: 0.65%

SIL tracks the Solactive Global Silver Miners, offering one of the highest-beta expressions of the silver mining industry. Its portfolio leans heavily toward established producers and royalty companies, giving it a reputation as the flagship silver miner ETF. Like other top miner ETFs, SIL tends to outperform in strong silver bull markets because of its greater exposure to companies with operational leverage and expansion potential. With more than ] $4 billion in assets under management, SIL offers the liquidity needed to buy or sell shares at a fair price, an important factor for all ETFs, but more so in the precious metals category.

While its dividend yield of 1.09% is not high, its 20%-plus dividend growth rate over the past three years indicates a focus on companies with a history of increasing dividends, signaling financial health and strong fundamentals. Thus, SIL offers a combination of high-growth potential, large size, and sound financial strength to help smooth out the otherwise volatile nature of precious metals miner funds. This provides some contrast to gold miners focusing on smaller market caps, like my next selection.

3. VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ)

  • AUM: $9.08B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Ranked in Asset Class (as of 12/3/2025): 6 out of 445
  • Ranked in Sub Class (as of 12/3/2025): 6 out of 12
  • Expense Ratio: 0.51%

GDXJ tracks the MVIS Global Junior Gold Miners Index, focusing on smaller, fast-growing gold exploration and production companies. Juniors historically deliver outsized torque to rising gold prices, and GDXJ remains the industry’s most liquid and widely followed vehicle for that exposure. Thus, it’s the higher-risk, higher potential return version of its larger cousin, the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX). Being miner ETFs, both will outperform the gold spot price (XAUUSD:CUR) in bull markets, with GDXJ edging out GDX.

GDXJ vs. GDX vs. Spot Gold: YTD Performance

What helps give GDXJ a boost is its earlier-stage miners and mid-tier producers, giving it a unique balance between extreme upside potential and somewhat less risk than pure micro-cap gold explorers. This makes this high-beta ETF an ideal precursor to another growth-oriented junior ETF.

4. Sprott Junior Gold Miners ETF (SGDJ)

  • AUM: $308.14M
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Ranked in Asset Class (as of 12/3/2025): 7 out of 445
  • Ranked in Sub Class (as of 12/3/2025): 7 out of 12
  • Expense Ratio: 0.50%

SGDJ tracks the Solactive Junior Gold Miners Custom Factors Index, which weights constituents on momentum and revenue growth rather than market cap. This factor-based construction distinguishes SGDJ from traditional junior-miner ETFs by concentrating on miners showing fundamental acceleration, not just size. This rules-based tilt helped SGDJ excel when growth-oriented miners led the sector. Furthermore, its expense ratio of 0.50%, its solid yield of 2.65%, plus a consistent dividend growth rate that includes a whopping 58.26% over five years, make SGDJ a compelling fund in the junior gold miner space.

SGDJ’s strategic tilt sets the stage for a comparison with a more senior miner ETF, whose five-year dividend growth (CAGR) is 23.81%.

5. iShares MSCI Global Gold Miners ETF (RING)

  • AUM: $2.75B
  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy
  • Ranked in Asset Class (as of 12/3/2025): 4 out of 445
  • Ranked in Sub Class (as of 12/3/2025): 4 out of 12
  • Expense Ratio: 0.39%

RING tracks the MSCI ACWI Select Gold Miners Investable Market Index, offering global exposure to senior mid-tier gold-mining companies in developed and emerging markets. Its strength lies in its pure-play construction, as the index includes only companies that derive the majority of revenue from gold mining. Its 0.39% expense ratio is lower than some peers, and its $2.75 billion AUM provides solid liquidity. RING appeals to investors seeking efficient, straightforward, and global leverage to gold prices. It often performs more steadily in the short term, while long-term performance has historically edged out its peers, making RING a more compelling holding for buy-and-hold investors.

RING vs. Gold Miner ETF Peers: 3-Year Performance

This well-rounded structure makes RING a fitting final comparison before turning to the broader conclusions of our silver and gold miner ETF showcase.

Silver and Gold Miner ETFs: High Risk-Return + Yield

Silver and gold have delivered extraordinary momentum in 2025, driven by soaring spot prices and supportive monetary signals, including a near-90% implied probability of a Dec. 10 rate cut and renewed investor appetite for inflation-resistant assets. The ETFs highlighted in this article – SLVP, SIL, GDXJ, SGDJ and RING – offer a range of approaches to capturing that momentum, from highly concentrated silver miner exposure to factor-tilted junior gold miners and globally diversified senior producers, while also offering an average five-year dividend growth rate (CAGR) of nearly 26%.

While miner ETFs can outperform their underlying metals during strong bull cycles, they also carry amplified downside risk, which is greater now as silver and gold trade near all-time highs. This reminds investors to size positions carefully and remain tactical. For those seeking leveraged participation in one of the year’s defining macro trades, these five funds represent some of the most compelling options in the precious metals universe. For investors wanting targeted exposure to specific miner stocks, check out my recent articles, including Crypto Stocks: As Good As Gold? and Gold Rush: Top 5 Gold Mining Stocks.

Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" quant stocks. Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing. If you’re looking for the latest deals to kick off the holidays, take a look at my recent article, Black Friday Deals: Top Stocks Under $10. Happy investing!

data.title: JOF: Probably Still Discounted, But Domestic Exposure Amid Yen Concerns
data.description:
Japan Smaller Capitalization Fund (JOF) offers a small-cap exposure to Japan. Price-to-Book Values, or PBRs, aren't as discounted anymore, which is a bit of a reason for concern given some of the return drivers possible in Japanese markets. What we do at least note is that despite exposure to more marginal businesses in the Japanese markets, the domestic exposure is not overwhelming—a lot of these businesses have considerable export markets.

Therefore, in light of the threats to the yen as the contradictory objectives between the monetary authority and the fiscal authority become more obvious, there is some mitigation in that quite a few sales made by the underlying holdings come from foreign markets. However, less than with the broader index. However, with the same ROE and sales growth rates as the category average, we still think that JOF is a decent value.

JOF Breakdown

The JOF portfolio is actively managed with higher expense ratios at 1.3%. It is quite skewed towards the top holdings, and the top 6 stocks include financial businesses with just domestic exposure, but also businesses with considerable foreign exposure, a plurality of exposure being the U.S., which is common for Japanese companies.

The importance of the exposure to foreign markets is the following: with quite a lot of Japanese industrial production still done in Japan, a weaker yen supports unit margins, with the top line being defended by mostly foreign FX-denominated exposure. While the broader index features automotive and industrial picks that have plentiful foreign exposure, JOF also gets this through its idiosyncratic exposures in Sakata INX and Nippon Seiki.

However, the first negative is that the JOF's exposure to foreign markets is less amid concerns for the valuation of the JPY. While holding out quite well against the USD, which has also been an abysmal performer in the FX markets, the JPY is dealing with a confluence of issues.

  • The BoJ has been looking to increase rates and normalize Japanese monetary policy. However, it's not necessarily the case that wage growth-driven inflation is there yet enough to allow for higher rates, which would support demand for the JPY.
  • Then there's the issue of the new administration's hope to drive the economy through fiscal support. This would jibe terribly with higher rates, as Japan has a massive debt-to-GDP, driven by immense domestic traditional institutional support in demanding government debt. Higher rates and higher debt threaten a currency crisis, even if rates would otherwise make saving in JPY marginally more interesting.

Conflicting monetary and fiscal objectives are an issue for the yen right now.

Bottom Line

The problem we have with the JOF is that expense ratios are higher, but there is more exposure to domestic business, which is yen-denominated. This isn't such a great setup even relative to the broader and more cheaply accessed index funds that at least provide a greater benefit from a weaker yen in export markets.

But there is still a value case for the JOF when looking at the headline figures. Sales growth is similar to the category average of funds like this, yet the PE and P/B are around 30% lower than category averages. Furthermore, based on the P/E * ROE = P/B relationship, we can also infer that ROEs are the same between the JOF and the category average. Given that therefore growth would have to be the explanation for why the P/Es are higher for the category average, but sales growth rates of the underlying are the same between the JOF and the category average, we'd directionally assume that the JOF is actually a decent deal.

However, we like even deeper P/B ratios in the <1x territory because there is usually more space for broad corporate governance and capital allocation improvements. Moreover, we have select ideas in these markets, albeit with a lot of domestic exposure, that we'd prefer without having to pay for the expertise of the JOF in the high expense ratio. Therefore, while JOF likely has some decently discounted stocks broadly speaking, we still aren't going for it considering the mitigating factors.

data.title: Datadog: The Rare Software Stock Defying The AI Reckoning
data.description:
It has become increasingly clear that the rise of AI will not be positive for all tech stocks. The software sector has seen great volatility, but Datadog, Inc. (DDOG) has bucked the trend. Whereas, many names have struggled to show the promised acceleration in growth rates, with weakness emerging instead. DDOG, on the other hand, has seen growth rates surge, and I expect continued momentum as the company is seeing strength across both AI and non-AI customers. The valuation remains an issue, with the stock trading at a large premium relative to peers and in its own right. Stay patient and wait for a better entry point.

DDOG Stock Price

I last covered DDOG in October, where I acknowledged the strong momentum but recommended avoiding the name due to valuation. The stock has been flat since then, which is a solid result amidst the reckoning in the software sector.

I am impressed by the continued momentum here, which was almost enough to bring back my bullishness for the name.

DDOG Stock Key Metrics

DDOG is an enterprise software company that handles everything data-related, especially observability. These include troubleshooting and performance analysis, which have become must-have products in the age of AI.

In the most recent quarter, DDOG generated 28.4% YoY revenue growth to $886 million, crushing guidance of between $847 million and $851 million. This was much higher than I anticipated by a wide margin.

The company operates in a highly competitive field with numerous competitors. Many years ago, I had feared that this might imply a saturated market, but the opposite is true: the market fragmentation represents opportunity for “consolidators” such as DDOG as customers have been flocking to product platforms over point solutions in recent years.

This is evidenced by the company’s strong dollar-based net retention rates, which hovered around 120% as the company continued seeing success cross-selling new products to existing customers.

The company was not profitable on a GAAP operational basis, but the loss was minimal, and I suspect it was due to aggressive investment in growth. The company did generate $207.4 million in non-GAAP operating profits, exceeding guidance of $176 million to $180 million. I expect the company to return to GAAP operating profitability next year.

DDOG ended the quarter with $4.1 billion of cash versus $1 billion of debt, representing a bulletproof net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided the fourth quarter to see up to $916 million in revenue, representing 24.1% YoY growth. I assume at least a modest beat, which would confirm that the robust growth is here to stay.

On the conference call, management called out a “notable inflection” in growth for its non-AI customers. Management noted that the “the sequential usage growth for non-AI existing customers was the highest we have seen going back 12 quarters.” Management reminded investors that the “digital transformation and cloud migration” story sometimes “slows down a little bit, but it accelerates after that,” and with the momentum having come back, they see it “going on for a very long time.” Management also cited strength in its AI customers, noting that this group now represents 12% of revenues, up from 11% in the second quarter. Management noted that the strong growth was not due to capitalizing more on GPUs, and thus that represents a “future opportunity.” The strong results explain in part why the stock has not been hit nearly as hard as others amidst the pain in the software sector.

Is DDOG Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

DDOG is establishing itself as a rare enterprise software winner in AI. That sounds like a weird thing to say just a couple of years into the rise of generative AI, but recent quarters have shown serious weakness in the software sector as investors are scrambling to determine which software names will be disrupted. My only issue with the stock is valuation. The stock recently traded at around 13x forward sales. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain healthy double-digit growth over the next handful of years.

That projected growth rate is likely to rank highly among software peers. Perhaps the reader might be wondering why this is not enough for a bullish rating. This is more evident when one looks at the stock on the basis of a price-to-earnings ratio. Consensus estimates call for the company to show strong earnings growth, but the stock is still trading at around 18x projected 2032 earnings.

There are a few ways to look at this. The easiest and quickest is to simply compare DDOG with other enterprise software names. Salesforce (CRM) is trading at under 5x forward sales. In contrast, DDOG was trading at around 5x 2032e sales. Yes, we have already established that DDOG looks like a clear winner from the rise of AI, but this appears to have led to an exorbitant premium to peers.

We can also judge the stock in its own right. I assume the company can achieve 35% net margins over the long term. That places the valuation at around 37x forward earnings, and 14x 2032e long-term earnings. Assuming the stock trades at around 30x long-term earnings in 2032, the stock is priced for around 11.5% annual returns over the next 7 years. That projected return may likely be enough to outperform the typical 8% return one would expect from the broader markets, but I would view both the underlying growth and valuation assumptions to be full and not offering much margin of safety. This explains why I am not quite at a "Sell" rating, as the stock is not nearly as egregiously overvalued as some other names in the tech sector.

DDOG Stock Conclusion

DDOG is offering accelerating top-line growth, with rates ranking among the top of peers alongside GAAP profitability. Growth investors might find that reason enough to buy the stock, but I remain concerned about the valuation. DDOG has many of the traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Neutral" rating for the stock.

data.title: Cracker Barrel: Shares Are Cheap Enough Even In Light Of Tough Times
data.description:
It always pains me to see a company that I have a great amount of emotional attachment to perform poorly. But sometimes, that's just how things work out in life. One such firm that has come under pressure from a share price perspective since I rated it a Buy back in June of 2024 is Cracker Barrel Old Country Store, Inc. (CBRL). Weak financial performance in the past, followed by a controversial decision to change their logo in what ultimately only proved to alienate some of its customer base, as well as the expectation of weakness moving forward, has pushed the stock price down 38.8% since that article was published. That compares to the 23.6% increase that the S&P 500 (SP500) has seen over the same window of time.

The good news is that not everything about the business is bad. Management is making a concerted effort to change things around. This includes pushing toward additional growth on the top and bottom lines. This year, management is actually optimistic that the business will achieve sales growth. But in the near term, analysts are anything but optimistic. Although the expectation from the analyst side of the equation is that the upcoming results (expected on December 9th) for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year will be disappointing, I think that the stock is cheap enough to justify the Buy rating I put on the stock previously.

Digging into Cracker Barrel Old Country Store

Ever since my first time visiting a Cracker Barrel Old Country Store, probably 25 years ago or so, I have loved what the business offers and what it represents. Its unique business model of offering a restaurant and a store under the same roof appealed to me. But it's more than just that. I have always appreciated the old-town aesthetic that defined the brand. And on top of that, I always found the food that the company offered to be the right mixture of quality, quantity, and price.

Even though the share price of the company has come under pressure in recent years, that has largely been because of a decline in profitability that management blamed on margin pressures, not on weakness from a sales perspective. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the last five completed fiscal years, starting in 2021 and ending in 2025. Revenue for the business has consistently increased. This rise has been driven primarily by an increase in the number of locations the business has in operation.

In the chart below, you can see the number of stores that it has operating for each of the fiscal years covered, as well as comparable sales. In the earlier years, comparable sales were rather strong. There was a dip in 2024, and the retail side of the business specifically has faced pressure over the last couple of years. But on the whole, the most recent data on that front has been encouraging as well. In 2025, for instance, comparable sales grew by 2.6%. Even though traffic dropped by 3%, check size grew by 6.5%.

Given the broader economic challenges that we are facing, with the cost of everything rising over the last few years, it's not surprising to see rising check sizes and declining guest traffic figures. Unfortunately, the company finds itself in a bit of a tough spot here. Normally, you would expect lower traffic in this kind of business and higher check sizes to boost margins. But the exact opposite has transpired. Back in 2021, the gross profit margin achieved by Cracker Barrel Old Country Store was 69.3%. By 2023, that declined to 67.2%.

The good news is that the price hikes for the business have pushed this back up to 69% by the end of the 2025 fiscal year. But other areas are proving to be more challenging. Labor and related costs, as an example, grew from 34.8% of revenue in 2021 to 36% in 2025. I know that doesn't sound like much of a difference. But when applied to the revenue generated during the 2025 fiscal year, that's an extra $41.8 million drop in pre-tax profits during that window. A lot of this pain came about between the 2023 and 2024 fiscal years, when store hourly labor costs jumped by 1.1% in relation to revenue because of higher staffing levels and wage inflation. This actually makes a lot of sense in and of itself. Unemployment throughout the U.S. was low during this time. The economy reopened rather quickly after the COVID-19 pandemic. And a surge in demand, which ultimately caused a lot of the inflation that we have seen, granted workers the greatest leverage in bargaining that they have had in likely decades.

Other cost items for the company worsened also. General and administrative costs grew from 5.2% of revenue to 6.2% over this window of time. That works out to an extra $34.8 million impact on the bottom line from a pre-tax perspective. Professional fees increased because of management’s investments in essentially restructuring the business for future growth, but there were other contributors as well, like payroll and related costs that grew. Regardless, the bottom-line impact here was anything but desirable.

There is some good news here. As I already mentioned earlier, comparable sales firmed up in 2025, even though traffic remained negative. Retail comparable sales fell less than they did the year before. And on top of that, the company has actually seen some improvement on its bottom line. Net income grew from $40.9 million in 2024 to $46.4 million in 2025. But that's not all. Operating cash flow jumped from $169 million to $218.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $268.3 million to $280.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $205.8 million to $224.3 million.

Clearly, management’s efforts to improve bottom-line results without sacrificing top-line growth are showing evidence of paying off. But recently, the company has become embroiled in controversy. Earlier this year, management decided to rebrand the business. This involved a very controversial change in its logo that the company made in order to modernize the concept. But public outcry was significant here. In short order, the firm decided to backtrack on this, but it's unclear to what extent this might come to haunt the business from an operational standpoint in the near term.

Management currently anticipates that revenue for 2026 will be between $3.35 billion and $3.45 billion. This is down, even at the high end, compared to the $3.48 billion the company reported for 2025. Although the number of locations in operation will likely grow during this time thanks to between $180 million and $220 million of capital spending, with $55 million to $95 million of that structured as strategic investments aimed at remodeling existing locations, trying out new technologies, and engaging in maintenance when it comes to existing assets, the firm is forecasting a 4% to 7% decline in traffic. This is based on the fact that, according to management, they believed that traffic during the first quarter of 2026 would be down by between 7% and 8%. Wage inflation is expected to bite on the bottom line, growing by between 3% and 4%. And commodity inflation should be between 2.5% and 3.5%. This all will result in menu price hikes of between 4% and 5%. Management is also investing a bit more in marketing, which is almost certainly in response to the backlash that it faced because of its attempted rebranding initiative.

Unfortunately, management expects that EBITDA for the 2026 fiscal year will be between $150 million and $190 million because of these changes. It's unclear what the longer-term ramifications are going to be from this change in guidance. Management mentioned when they last announced financial results that their expectations for 2027 have changed as well, though from what I can see, there have not been any definitive public revisions to their belief that revenue will grow to between $3.8 billion and $3.9 billion by that year, with EBITDA expanding to between $375 million and $425 million. Given that the previous expectation for 2026 had been revenue of between $3.4 billion and $3.5 billion, with EBITDA anticipated to be between $200 million and $215 million, it's likely that 2027 will be revised lower also.

The good news is that management has been working on cutting costs. Earlier this year, the company said that it was working on certain cost savings and margin optimization initiatives aimed at improving bottom-line results by between $50 million and $60 million on an annualized basis through the end of the 2027 fiscal year. But now, the most recent estimates there have been are for savings of between $55 million and $60 million, so at the midpoint, management is stepping things up a bit. It does appear as though its decision to close 14 Maple Street units is playing a role here.

In the chart above, you can see how shares of the company are priced using the original estimates for 2027, as well as the revised estimate for 2026 and results for 2024 and 2025. As much as I would love it if the firm could deliver on the 2027 estimate still, even if it doesn't, shares are priced at incredibly low levels. And in fact, relative to most other similar firms, the stock is cheap also. As the table below illustrates, only two of the five firms that I compared it to on a price-to-operating cash flow basis are cheaper than it is. And when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, it is the cheapest of the group.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Cracker Barrel Old Country Store 2.2 4.7
Brinker International (EAT) 8.0 9.1
Kura Sushi USA (KRUS) 22.0 56.0
Bloomin' Brands (BLMN) 1.9 8.6
Red Robin Gourmet Burgers (RRGB) 2.0 8.7
Darden Restaurants (DRI) 11.3 14.0

Naturally, the picture can change as new data comes in. And that new data is expected after the market closes on December 9th. The bad thing here is that, as the table below illustrates, revenue, earnings, and adjusted earnings are all forecasted to fall. Analysts do not provide public estimates of cash flows. But it is highly likely that those will contract also if these bottom-line expectations that analysts have provided turn out to materialize.

Takeaway

The way I see it, Cracker Barrel Old Country Store has a history over the last few years now of running into challenges. But the good news is that, through it all, the company has consistently grown revenue. This year, things are looking a little different, and not in a positive way. But management is well aware of these issues and is working in order to address them. Because of how cheap CBRL shares are and because management remains confident in delivering on cost-cutting opportunities, I do believe that maintaining a Buy rating is appropriate.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Atmus Filtration Technologies: Resorting To M&A To Accelerate Growth
data.description:
Shares of Atmus Filtration Technologies Inc. (ATMU) have rapidly built up an impressive track record, proving my cautious stance on the business wrong.

The filtration business, which formerly belonged to Cummins (CMI), has been successful since it became an independent business, despite arguably some challenges with regard to its positioning from the start.

Having demonstrated slow but solid growth, Atmus is now resorting to M&A moves to move away from its key on- and off-highway filtration products. This makes me more upbeat on the business, but with shares having re-rated to a 20 times earnings multiple, while some leverage is apparent, I am turning very cautious here.

All About Filtration

Atmus Filtration was spun off from engine maker Cummins in a separation that took place in the spring of 2023. The company has a mission to protect what is important, something that the business does by providing leading both on-highway and off-highway filtration products.

The business was founded in 1958, with global operations spanning 10 manufacturing facilities, 5 technical centers, employing about 4,500 workers, which generate about $1.7 billion in sales. Roughly half these revenues are generated in North America, complemented by a roughly equally large revenue contribution in Latin America, EMEA, and the Asia-Pacific region.

The on-highway segment makes up about 60% of sales, this including products like coolants, chemicals, ventilation, lubricants, and fuel. Clients include major OEMs like PACCAR, Daimler, Volvo, etc.

The remaining 40% of sales are generated off-highway, including coolants, hydraulics, crankcase ventilation, lubes, crankcases, etc., with clientele including Cummins, Traton, Komatsu, Doosan, and John Deere, among others. These include vehicles used in agriculture, construction, mining, and power generation.

The company operates in global addressable markets pegged at $70 billion per annum, with addressable markets seen at roughly 20% of these numbers. These generally are mission-critical products and consumables in stable and growing end markets, yet at the time of the separation, I arguably have been a bit too fearful about the electrification overhang in these markets.

That, however, was the long-term threat that might render some of its products useless. At the same time, more stringent emission regulations made it so that near-term demand for such filters and products would be on the increase, creating somewhat of a tough balancing act.

Delivering

Atmus went public at $19.50 per share in May 2023, levels at which the business at large was valued at $2.2 billion. With sales trending around $1.6 billion at the time, EBITDA seen near a quarter of a billion, and with earnings power seen around $1.50 per share, overall valuations looked very fair.

The company started to deliver soon thereafter, after consecutive guidance hikes. In the end, the company reported a 4% increase in 2023 sales to $1.63 billion, with GAAP earnings reported at $2.05 per share and adjusted earnings reported at $2.31 per share.

The company guided for flattish results in 2024, seeing sales at around $1.64 billion, with adjusted earnings seen around $2.225 per share, indicating complete flattish results. With the company largely reiterating that guidance halfway through the year, a $34 stock had re-rated from a low-teen multiple to a mid-teen multiple, with leverage seen around 1.5 times EBITDA.

I believed that the real opportunity was gone given the re-rating in the share price, which made me cautious and left me concluding that shares did not pass my own filter, unfortunately with the benefit of hindsight.

Moving Ahead

Shares of Atmus have actually traded largely flattish in the year since I offered my last take on the shares, having largely traded in a $30-$40 range in the first half of the year before moving higher to the $45 mark in the fall, now trading up to a high of $52 per share, driven by positive news flow.

In the meantime, Atmus reported a 2% and change increase in 2024 sales to $1.67 billion. Adjusted earnings rose by nineteen cents to $2.50 per share, with GAAP earnings improving by similar numbers to $2.22 per share. The company guided for modest inroads in 2025, with sales seen up to a midpoint of $1.70 billion and adjusted earnings again seen as flattish between $2.35 and $2.60 per share.

Shares moved higher after the company hiked the full-year earnings guidance in a modest fashion following the release of the second-quarter results as well as the third-quarter results. As of the third quarter, the midpoint of the full-year sales guidance is now seen at over $1.73 billion, with earnings seen between $2.50 and $2.65 per share.

Net debt of $359 million is basically at par compared to the EBITDA guidance of $342 million for the year, and given the continued and modest beat in the guidance, through which Atmus is rapidly building up a solid track record, the re-rating continues. At $45, shares have re-rated to 17-18 times anticipated earnings this year, with leverage ratios seen around 1 times.

With 82 million shares trading at $45, the equity valuation stood at $3.7 billion, for a more than $4.0 billion enterprise valuation. This valued Atmus at 2.3 times sales, about 12 times EBITDA, and 17-18 times earnings.

A Substantial Deal

After a decent but not spectacular operating performance, Atmus has resorted to M&A moves as well. Towards the end of November, Atmus reached a deal to acquire Koch Filter Corp. in a $450 million deal.

This deal is valued at just over 10% of its own valuation and involves the addition of a producer of essential air filtration solutions for industrial, HVAC, data centers, and power generation applications. Given a $156 million revenue contribution, the purchase price comes at a premium, valued at 2.9 times sales. However, given the run-up to the low-twenties, the sales multiple of standalone Atmus has risen to 2.6 times as well.

The purchase price is equivalent to 13.9 times EBITDA, suggesting a $32 million EBITDA contribution, indicating that these EBITDA margins are seen at 20% and change in relation to sales, up about a point compared to Atmus itself. Factoring in tax synergies, the purchase price drops to $395 million, which, combined with cost synergies, reduces the purchase in terms of EBITDA multiple to 10.9 times.

Net debt will double to about $800 million, and with pro forma EBITDA seen close to $400 million, leverage ratios are reasonable at around 2 times. The deal will create a third segment outside of vehicles, with industrial air making up 8% of pro forma industrial sales. This could offset some of the headwinds from the electrification headwind and improve diversification at large, with the deal being accretive in 2026, although the extent of which has not been quantified.

Assuming a similar 2% D&A intensity as Atmus in relation to sales, a $32 million EBITDA contribution suggests a $29 million EBIT contribution. Factoring in a 5% cost of debt on a $450 million price, pre-tax profits could jump by $7 million. This could boost post-tax earnings by about $5 million, or about six cents, based on a share tally of 82 million shares. However, synergies of $4 million over time could boost earnings by another four cents, combined with the potential to boost earnings if we factor in deleveraging, creating a real roadmap for earnings to move up towards $2.75 per share next year.

And Now?

Following the momentum coming out of the third quarter earnings report and purchase of Koch, shares of Atmus have risen to fresh highs around $52 per share here. This frankly makes me cautious, as multiples have re-rated to 20 times earnings seen this year, and while the multiple for next year might come down a turn, leverage ratios have risen overnight from about 1 times to 2 times EBITDA as well.

All this makes me rather cautious here, similar to before, but this time really cautious, as the re-rating is more than complete, which, in combination with still a challenged long-term positioning and now some additional debt, makes me very cautious. It seems as if the Atmus re-rating is more than complete, as while the reported growth is solid, it is nothing spectacular here.
